screenshot_note: Puppeteer screenshots could not be captured this cycle due to system resource exhaustion (zombie chromium processes from prior agent sessions prevent new thread creation). Review is based on full source code analysis (Office.jsx, App.jsx, Office.css, App.css) and the R19 reference screenshots. Source files were last modified at 13:32 UTC — after the R19 review was written — confirming zero code changes between R19 and R20. The deployed bundle hash on https://osortega.github.io/bloberto-office/ (index-DwG5Afbh.js) matches the current source tree.

carries_from_r19.shortcut_toast_mobile: NOT FIXED — fifth consecutive review. No `@media (hover: none) and (pointer: coarse)` rule exists anywhere in App.css. The `.shortcut-toast` class block (App.css lines 1045–1064) has no mobile guard. The ShortcutToast component also has no touch detection. First-time mobile visitors still see keyboard shortcut hints that are meaningless on touchscreens. Fix is a single CSS rule. This has been flagged HIGH since review #16.
carries_from_r19.completion_toast_aria_live: NOT FIXED — App.jsx lines 945–969: `{completionToast && <div aria-live='polite'>...}` still conditionally mounts. Newly-mounted aria-live regions with content already present will not be announced by NVDA, JAWS, or VoiceOver in default modes. Same code, same bug, fifth review.
carries_from_r19.office_jsx_monolith: NOT FIXED — Office.jsx is still exactly 1218 lines. The file did not shrink or split since R19. CharacterAvatar (~250 lines of per-worker SVG), WallClock, ConferenceTable, VibeShockwave, and AwaySign are all still inlined. No `src/components/` directory was created.
carries_from_r19.stable_activity_log_noop: NOT FIXED — App.jsx line 759: `const stableActivityLog = useMemo(() => activityLog, [activityLog])` unchanged. activityLogLenRef dead code still present on line 758.
carries_from_r19.workercard_memo_defeated: NOT FIXED — `activityEntries={stableActivityLog}` still passed to every WorkerCard at four call sites (App.jsx lines 1058, 1083, 1106, 1119). stableActivityLog produces a new reference on every 30s poll. React.memo on WorkerCard does nothing.

suggestions.title: ShortcutToast STILL renders on mobile — FIVE reviews in, no excuse left
suggestions.description: No mobile guard has been added in five consecutive reviews. First-time visitors on mobile (375px viewport) still see '⌨️ Shortcuts: 1 Office · 2 Dashboard · R Refresh · Esc Clear' — keyboard shortcuts for a device with no keyboard. The fix is literally one CSS rule: `@media (hover: none) and (pointer: coarse) { .shortcut-toast { display: none; } }` in App.css. Alternatively, detect touch capability in the ShortcutToast component: `const isTouch = window.matchMedia('(hover: none) and (pointer: coarse)').matches` and short-circuit early. Either approach takes under 5 minutes. The toast has localStorage dismiss-on-first-use, so once a mobile user sees it, they won't see it again — but that's not an excuse to not fix it.

suggestions.title: THREE conditional aria-live mounts — completionToast, plantToast, and ShortcutToast all broken
suggestions.description:
This is now worse than R19 because the count grew from 1 to 3: (1) completionToast in App.jsx line 945: `{completionToast &&
}` — reported since R18. (2) plantToast in Office.jsx line 918: `{plantToast &&
}` — same antipattern in a different file, unnoticed until this review. (3) ShortcutToast returns null when hidden, then mounts a div with aria-live='polite' already containing text — same behavior (App.jsx line 543). All three fail for the same reason: aria-live regions must exist in the DOM BEFORE content is added to them. A newly-mounted element with content is not reliably announced by NVDA, JAWS, or VoiceOver. Fix pattern (apply to all three): create a single persistent `
` at the root of App, then imperatively set its textContent when toasts fire, clear it when they close. One fix, three bugs resolved.

suggestions.title: Office.jsx still 1218 lines — unchanged from R19, unaddressed since R18
suggestions.description: The file is at exactly 1218 lines — same count as R19. Nothing was extracted. To be concrete about what to split: CharacterAvatar is a pure memo component with 250 lines of per-worker SVG art that has zero dependencies on office state. WallClock is a standalone 60s-interval widget. ConferenceTable is self-contained. VibeShockwave is 30 lines. AwaySign is 35 lines. Extracting these five into `src/components/` would bring Office.jsx to roughly 600 lines — a manageable single file. This is not a style preference; at 1200+ lines, finding any bug requires grep or scrolling past hundreds of lines of unrelated SVG art.

suggestions.title: WorkerCard React.memo still defeated on every 30s poll — stableActivityLog still a no-op
suggestions.description: App.jsx line 759: `const stableActivityLog = useMemo(() => activityLog, [activityLog])`. This has the same reference instability as plain `activityLog` — useMemo with a dependency that changes every poll doesn't stabilize anything. All four WorkerCard call sites pass `activityEntries={stableActivityLog}`. React.memo compares props by reference; stableActivityLog is a new reference every poll; every WorkerCard re-renders every 30 seconds in lockstep. The memo wrapper is purely aspirational. Fix: change the dependency to `[activityLog.length]` so the reference only changes when new entries arrive (O(1) check). Or use a separate `activityByWorker` useMemo keyed on the worker's own entries only. Either way, the current code is misleading — it looks like there's optimization but there isn't.

suggestions.title: Stuck worker stat card is click-only — no keyboard support
suggestions.description: NEW FINDING. App.jsx lines 425–440: the `stat-card--stuck` div scrolls to the stuck worker on click, which is a useful affordance. But it's a bare `div` with no `role`, no `tabIndex`, and no `onKeyDown`. Keyboard users have no way to trigger this navigation. Fix: add `role='button'`, `tabIndex={0}`, and `onKeyDown={(e) => { if (e.key === 'Enter' || e.key === ' ') { e.preventDefault(); scrollToWorker() } }}`. Three attributes, one function reference. This is a common 'forgot to make a div keyboard-accessible' mistake.

suggestions.title: vibeHistory sparkline initializes empty — no localStorage hydration on mount
suggestions.description: NEW FINDING. App.jsx line 581: `const [vibeHistory, setVibeHistory] = useState([])`. The sparkline shows nothing on initial render until the first successful poll resolves and triggers the teamVibe.key useEffect (which then reads localStorage and sets vibeHistory). For a returning user, this means the sparkline flashes empty for up to 30 seconds on every page load even though full history is available in localStorage immediately. Fix: hydrate eagerly — `useState(() => { try { const raw = localStorage.getItem('bloberto-vibe-history'); return raw ? JSON.parse(raw) : [] } catch { return [] } })`. Three-line change, instant history on mount.

suggestions.title: Ghost characters are keyboard-focusable but non-interactive — accessibility trap (carry from R19)
suggestions.description: Office.jsx line 508: `tabIndex={0}` is unconditional on every Character div. Ghost variant characters (OOO roster members rendered at empty desks) have no onClick handler and no keyboard handler. Pressing Enter or Space on a focused ghost character does nothing but the element has `tabIndex={0}` signaling it is interactive. Fix: `tabIndex={handleClick ? 0 : -1}`. Ghost characters still show their hover tooltip on mouse over, but keyboard-only users are better off skipping them entirely.

suggestions.title: activityLogLenRef is dead code — set but never read (carry from R19)
suggestions.description: App.jsx line 758: `const activityLogLenRef = useRef(0)`. Line 760: `useEffect(() => { activityLogLenRef.current = activityLog.length }, [activityLog.length])`. This ref is never read anywhere in the file. It is a leftover from a previous optimization attempt that was superseded. Delete both lines. In an 1199-line file, dead code creates cognitive overhead — readers assume there is a purpose they must discover.

suggestions.title: Duplicate inline toast style objects across App.jsx and Office.jsx — should be a CSS class
suggestions.description: There are now THREE nearly-identical inline style objects for toasts: completionToast (App.jsx 948-969), plantToast (Office.jsx 918-942), and they share all structural properties — `position: fixed`, `bottom: 2rem`, `left: 50%`, `transform: translateX(-50%)`, `borderRadius: 12px`, `padding: 0.6rem 1.2rem`, `fontSize: 0.95rem`, `fontWeight: 600`, `zIndex: 9999`, `whiteSpace: nowrap`, `pointerEvents: none`, `animation: badge-pop`. Only border color differs. Extract a `.toast-popup` CSS class in App.css. Inline styles break CSS theming (light mode can't override inline `color`) and prevent GPU batching hints.

suggestions.title: Wander animation classes cap at 4 — 5th+ idle worker gets path collision (carry from R19)
suggestions.description: Office.jsx line 1133: `const wIdx = (i % 4) + 1`. With 5 non-manager roster members, an all-idle state is reachable. Worker 5 gets `char--wander-1` (same as worker 1), same path, same IDLE_BUBBLE_EMOJIS[1] ('☕'). Two workers moving identically breaks the illusion of an organic office. Fix: define `char--wander-5` in Office.css with a new keyframe path, and update the cap from 4 to 5.

suggestions.title: Worker filter bar silently shows empty when API returns flat array format (carry from R19)
suggestions.description: App.jsx line 1163: per-worker activity filter buttons are built from `roster.map(...)`. When the data source returns a flat array instead of `{workers, roster}`, `roster` is set to `[]` (line 678) and the filter row renders only 'All Workers' with no individual buttons — a silent feature loss. There is no explanatory message and no fallback. Fix: either derive roster from the workers array as a fallback (deduplicated by name), or hide the entire worker filter row with a meaningful note when roster is empty.

suggestions.title: Worker card flip doesn't move focus to Close button (carry from R19)
suggestions.description: Clicking ℹ️ flips the card, but focus stays on the now-hidden ℹ️ button. Keyboard users must Tab-hunt through the flipped card's contents to find the Close button. Fix: `useEffect(() => { if (isFlipped) cardRef.current?.querySelector('.worker-card__back-close')?.focus() }, [isFlipped])`. Two lines in WorkerCard. The inverse (returning focus to ℹ️ on close) is already handled by the browser naturally since that button regains visibility.

suggestions.title: Activity filter uses role=radio on <button> elements — ambiguous to screen readers (carry from R19)
suggestions.description: App.jsx lines 1136–1173: `<button role='radio' aria-checked={...}>` is a conflicting ARIA pattern. A `<button>` already has an implicit role of 'button'; overlaying `role='radio'` creates a mixed signal — screen readers may announce these as 'radio button button'. The correct approach for this toggle-filter pattern is either: (a) use `role='tab'` / `role='tablist'` since the behavior is tab-like with mutual exclusivity, or (b) use `<input type='radio'>` with associated labels. The radiogroup itself (`<div role='radiogroup'>`) is fine; the conflict is the button element.

suggestions.title: 118 keyframe declarations — no GPU compositing hints on continuously animated elements
suggestions.description: Office.css now has 90 @keyframes declarations; App.css has 28 more — 118 total, unchanged from R19. Elements that animate continuously (ambient-particle, wander paths, ghost-float, char--working bob) lack `will-change: transform`. Without compositing hints, the browser may repaint these on the CPU per frame. On mobile this contributes to jank. A targeted audit — add `will-change: transform` to the 4–5 elements that are ALWAYS animating — would reduce paint cost without restructuring the animation system.

suggestions.title: Office window daytime gradient is near-white against dark floor (carry from R19)
suggestions.description: WindowElement renders `linear-gradient(to bottom, #bae6fd, #f0f9ff)` during daytime hours (9am–4pm). Against the dark office floor (`--bg: #05050f`), this is visually jarring — it reads as a blown-out rectangle rather than a sky view. Slightly tinting the endpoints would help: `#93c5fd → #dbeafe` maintains 'sky blue' while reducing the brightness delta. Alternatively, add a subtle `border: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.15)` and a mild `opacity: 0.9` to integrate it with the overall dark aesthetic.

whats_good: The AwaySign component (Office.jsx line 331) is a delightful implementation — a tiny sticky-note SVG with a folded corner, ruled lines, and message text, with data-tier coloring for short/mid/long absences. The seed-based message selection ensures the same worker always gets the same note for a given idle duration, which is consistent and charming.

char--deep-work is properly defined in Office.css with three distinct effects: a golden glow filter, indigo halo animation on the avatar, and a 🎧 pseudo-element appended to the name. The feature triggers at 45-minute thresholds, which is a reasonable deep-work heuristic.

Ghost character OOO bubble (Office.jsx lines 520–535) shows role + 'Last seen: Xm ago' on hover, computed in real time from activityEntries. This is exactly the right information density for an OOO state.

WallClock vibe behaviors are subtle and excellent: frantic tick animation class in 'crushing' mode and a 2s CSS ease transition for 'slow-day'. These are purely cosmetic but reinforce the emotional register of each vibe.

The desk-specific screen SVGs (carlos code lines, maya wireframe grid, dave blinking cursor, sofia bar chart, luna color blocks) remain one of the best craft details in the project. Per-worker art at this level of fidelity is rare in a side project.

Polling pause on 10-min inactivity remains well-implemented — event listeners on mousemove, keydown, and touchstart all resume polling. The pollingPaused pill in the header gives appropriate user feedback.
The reduce-motion coverage is still outstanding — 12 separate @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) blocks across the CSS. This is well above average for a project of this type.

Solo spotlight layout (workers-grid--solo) shows when only one active worker is present, with a 'Carrying the whole team' message. It's a thoughtful edge-case treatment that could easily have been ignored.
summary: Score DOWN again: 3.5 → 3.4. All five carries from R19 remain unresolved. Zero code changes were made between R19 and R20 — the source files are byte-for-byte identical. This review introduces two new significant findings: (1) a THIRD aria-live antipattern in plantToast (Office.jsx line 918), bringing the total to three broken screen-reader announcements in one codebase; (2) the stuck-worker stat card is click-only with no keyboard support. Accessibility drops to 2/5 this cycle — not because the craft is bad, but because a known class of bug has now proliferated to three call sites without being addressed. The fun factor holds at 5 and the visual craft remains genuinely impressive. But a scoreboard where the fun score carries the average while accessibility falls is exactly backwards for a product people should be able to use.
